coredata.dc:title: Two-in-one platform based on conjugated polymer for ultrasensitive ratiometric detection and efficient removal of perfluoroalkyl substances from environmental water

coredata.dc:description: Continuous emergence of persistent organic pollutants perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctane sulfonic acid (PFOS) in various water bodies around the world poses a serious threat to the global ecosystem. The exploration of advanced detection/removal techniques to monitor/treat such type of toxicants is urgently required. Herein, we unveiled a donor-acceptor type conjugated polymer PF-DBT-Im as a first-of-its-kind ratiometric fluorescent probe for visual, amplified, and specific monitoring of PFOA and PFOS with ultra-low detection limits of 6.12 nM (PFOA) and 14.3 nM (PFOS), respectively. PF-DBT-Im undergoes strong aggregation after binding with PFOA/PFOS as evident by transmission electron microscopy, zeta potential measurements, and dynamic light scattering studies. This promotes interchain Förster resonance energy transfer process to endorse an obvious emission color change from blue-to-magenta under ultraviolet lamp excitation. Consequently, a smartphone-integrated portable device is fabricated for realizing rapid and on-site detection of PFOA/PFOS. Besides, a new class of magnetic adsorbent Fe3O4@NH2&F13 is also prepared and used in combination with PF-DBT-Im to remove PFOA/PFOS from the environmental water effectively and rapidly as confirmed by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry analysis. Thus, utilizing the excellent signal amplification property of PF-DBT-Im and the remarkable magnetic separation capability of Fe3O4@NH2&F13, a multifunctional system is developed for step-wise recognition and separation of PFOA/PFOS from the environmental water proficiently and rapidly.

PL spectra of PF-DBT-Im (30 μM) with successive additions of (a) PFOA and (c) PFOS. Inset: corresponding changes in the color of solution under UV light. Stern-Volmer plots for (b) PFOA and (d) PFOS, respectively.

UV–visible spectra depicting changes in the absorption peak of PF-DBT-Im (30 μM) upon the addition of (a) PFOA (30 μM) and (b) PFOS (50 μM), respectively. Changes in the (c) zeta potential and (d) size of PF-DBT-Im (30 μM) after the addition of PFOA (30 μM) and PFOS (50 μM).

The fluorescence response of PF-DBT-Im towards various (a) metal ions, (b) anions, surfactants, other coexistences in water, and (c) some PFASs analogues. Concentration of PF-DBT-Im and other analytes were 30 and 50 μM, respectively.

(a) Smartphone-integrated portable testing device. The images of the PF-DBT-Im (30 μM) solutions with various concentrations of (b) PFOA and (c) PFOS along with corresponding calibration plots obtained using R/B values.
The synthetic route of Fe3O4@NH2&F13 and mechanism of magnetic removal of PF-DBT-Im/PFOA or PF-DBT-Im/PFOS using Fe3O4@NH2&F13.

(b) The photographs showing change in color of PF-DBT-Im solution after addition of PFOA/PFOS and Fe3O4@NH2&F13 under day light and UV light. LC-MS analysis validating removal capability of (b) PFOA and (c) PFOS by Fe3O4@NH2&F13. The initial concentrations (yellow line) of PFOA and PFOS were 30 μM and 50 μM, respectively.
A comparative study of some reported fluorescence sensors with our work for the detection of PFOA and PFOS.

head.ce:title: Two-in-one platform based on conjugated polymer for ultrasensitive ratiometric detection and efficient removal of perfluoroalkyl substances from environmental water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Continuous emergence of persistent organic pollutants perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctane sulfonic acid (PFOS) in various water bodies around the world poses a serious threat to the global ecosystem. The exploration of advanced detection/removal techniques to monitor/treat such type of toxicants is urgently required. Herein, we unveiled a donor-acceptor type conjugated polymer PF-DBT-Im as a first-of-its-kind ratiometric fluorescent probe for visual, amplified, and specific monitoring of PFOA and PFOS with ultra-low detection limits of 6.12 nM (PFOA) and 14.3 nM (PFOS), respectively. PF-DBT-Im undergoes strong aggregation after binding with PFOA/PFOS as evident by transmission electron microscopy, zeta potential measurements, and dynamic light scattering studies. This promotes interchain Förster resonance energy transfer process to endorse an obvious emission color change from blue-to-magenta under ultraviolet lamp excitation. Consequently, a smartphone-integrated portable device is fabricated for realizing rapid and on-site detection of PFOA/PFOS. Besides, a new class of magnetic adsorbent Fe3O4@NH2&F13 is also prepared and used in combination with PF-DBT-Im to remove PFOA/PFOS from the environmental water effectively and rapidly as confirmed by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry analysis. Thus, utilizing the excellent signal amplification property of PF-DBT-Im and the remarkable magnetic separation capability of Fe3O4@NH2&F13, a multifunctional system is developed for step-wise recognition and separation of PFOA/PFOS from the environmental water proficiently and rapidly.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) represent a class of man-made organic compounds that have been widely used in the preparation of industrial, commercial, and consumer products, such as non-stick cookware, food packaging material, stain-repellants, foam extinguishers, etc. (Glüge et al., 2020). Among many PFASs, perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) are the most common hazardous materials (Zhao et al., 2016) and categorized as emerging pollutants (Podder et al., 2021) because they are constantly found in a variety of natural water systems around the globe, including surface water, groundwater, and even drinking water (Kurwadkar et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2019a). For example, the concentration of PFOA and PFOS in the electroplating industrial wastewater is reported to be as high as 25,016 ng L−1, which even affected the artificial lake 10 km away from the site (Tang et al., 2019). The total concentration of PFOS and PFOA is reported to reach 1174 μg L−1 in the wastewater of industrial areas (Wang et al., 2010). Worst of all, the concentration of PFOA in drinking water near a fluorochemical facility in Washington, West Virginia, USA, was found to be as high as 13.3 μg L−1 (Hu et al., 2016). Besides, these PFASs can easily bioaccumulate and biomagnify in humans as well as wildlife, eliciting a plethora of health hazards including thyroid disease, visceral injury, immune deficiency, reproductive toxicity, and cancer (Sunderland et al., 2019; Fenton et al., 2021). Above all, these pollutants are resistant to thermal, chemical, and biological decomposition due to the presence of strong CF bonds (485 kJ/mol), making them a persistent chemical threat to the environment as well as humans (Lu et al., 2020). Therefore, considering the recent environmental and health crisis, the rapid, sensitive and on-site monitoring of PFOA/PFOS along with their efficient elimination from the environmental water is highly imperative and greatly needed.

The conventional detection method for PFOA/PFOS is liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), which shows satisfactory specificity and sensitivity (Amin et al., 2020). However, this method has limited practical applications due to the requirement of expensive equipment, well-trained technicians, difficult sample preparation procedure, and time-consuming process. Moreover, LC-MS is unsuitable for speedy real-time detection of PFASs at remote locations or desired places. In this regard, fluorescent sensors which can sensitively and quickly detect the analytes based on the change in fluorescence signal are advantageous and attracted extensive attention by the researchers due to their simplicity, portability, and relatively low-cost. Recent years have witnessed numerous smart fluorescent materials for the detection of a variety of chemical and biological analytes (Rasheed et al., 2018; Dou et al., 2022; He et al., 2022; Han et al., 2022). Among them, conjugated polymers (CPs) containing highly delocalized backbone structure have emerged as elite fluorescent material over small molecule counterparts owing to their astonishing light-harvesting properties and excellent sensitivity due to the unique phenomenon of “molecular-wire effect” (Swager, 1998; Thomas et al., 2007; Pak et al., 2021). Surprisingly, no detection platform based on CP has been established so far for the recognition of PFOA/PFOS. This motivates us to develop an ultrasensitive probe for the detection of PFOA/PFOS using signal amplified fluorescent CP material.

Though, some reasonable fluorescent probes based on nanoparticles (Walekar et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2020), dyes (Cheng et al., 2018) and supramolecules (Zheng et al., 2019a) have been reported for monitoring PFOA/PFOS, yet, many of them suffer from several inevitable problems like low sensitivity, inadequate selectivity, on-site detection inability, and post-detection removal incapability. Moreover, in most of the reported sensors, the detection has been performed by monitoring the change in the single emission band, which is highly prone to erroneous results caused by instrumental fluctuation, or environmental effects. Few sincere attempts have also been made to design ratiometric fluorescent probes for the detection of PFOA/PFOS, however on the verge of using additional fluorophore as a label. For example, Tan's research group has proposed a hybrid ratiometric nanosensor for the detection of PFOS utilizing fluorescent dye ethidium bromide (EB) as the reference signal label and the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as the signal responsive label (Chen et al., 2020). Guo et al. elegantly constructed a molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent probe for PFOS by physical mixing of two kinds of upconversion nanoparticles designated as a reference and signal responsive label (Tian et al., 2021). Unfortunately, such probes necessitate the use of two different fluorophores for achieving ratiometric detection which not only upsurges the experimental cost but also makes the system complicated. Thus, to circumvent the existing drawbacks, the development of a simple, label-free fluorescent probe based on signal amplified CP for ultrasensitive, ratiometric and site-specific detection of PFOA/PFOS would be novel and indispensable in the present context.

The efficient removal of PFOA/PFOS from the natural water systems after precise detection is equally important and needs urgent attention in view of the increasing environmental pollution and human toxicity. Since the degradation of these pollutants by artificial or natural processes is extremely challenging, their elimination by an economical and effective process of adsorption is a promising alternative. The most common adsorbents of PFOA/PFOS include activated carbon (Cantoni et al., 2021), carbon nanoparticles (Fabregat-Palau et al., 2022), resins (Liu et al., 2022), and metal-organic framework (Pauletto and Bandosz, 2022) which exhibits a pervasive issue of the poor selectivity and difficult separation process. To overcome such shortcomings in the adsorption process, recently, our group has proposed a surface functionalization strategy for designing adsorbents by modifying the surface of magnetic substrates with functional groups tailored to the characteristics of target molecules (Hao et al., 2021). It has been established that targets can be easily separated using a simple magnet within a few seconds with enhanced adsorption capacity as well as selectivity due to unique and highly cooperative adsorption interaction between structurally similar targeting and surface functionalized groups. This becomes our source of encouragement for the development of a similar adsorbent by surface functionalization strategy for the post-detection immediate removal of PFOA/PFOS from the water bodies through simple magnetic separation which would be innovative and worthwhile for restricting primary as well as secondary pollution.

In this work, we present an unprecedented two-in-one platform for hypersensitive detection and magnetic separation of emerging pollutants PFOA/PFOS from the environmental water employing color-tunable CP (PF-DBT-Im) as signal responsive material and surface-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4@NH2&F13) as smart adsorbent. The aqueous solution of PF-DBT-Im displayed blue-to-magenta transformation in its emission color exclusively after the addition of PFOA/PFOS which facilitates amplified and naked eye ratiometric monitoring of these pollutants for the first time using CP material. Interestingly, the detection system was further extended to portable platform using smartphone equipped handy device for realizing prompt and real-time estimation of PFOA/PFOS at desirable places. Finally, the surface-modified iron nanoparticles Fe3O4@NH2&F13 which carry certain structural similarities to PFOS/PFOA were utilized as admirable adsorbent in combination with PF-DBT-Im for magnetic removal of PFASs instantaneously and robustly. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of utilizing conjugated polymer material in combination with magnetic adsorbent for sensitive detection as well as magnetic removal of emerging pollutants PFOA/PFOS from the environmental water proficiently and rapidly.

1,6-Dibromohexane (97 %), 2,7-dibromofluorene (98 %), bis(pinacolato)diboron (99 %), 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorooctyltriethoxysilane (PFOTES, 98 %), 1-methylimidazole (99 %), perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorobutyric acid (PFBA), perfluorobutane sulfonate(PFBS), butyric acid (BA), 1-butanesulfonic acid (BSA), octaneoic acid (OA), octanesulfonic acid (OSA), (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES, 99 %), 4,7-Bis(5-bromo-2-thienyl)-2,1,3-benzothiadiazole (M3, 95 %), and tetrakis(triphenylphosphine)palladium(0) (Pd-8.9 %) [Pd(PPh3)4] were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) was obtained from J&K Scientific (Beijing, China). Estradiol (E2), dioctyl phthalate (DOP), bisphenol A (BPA), sulfamerazine free acid (SM1), tetracycline (TYC), 4-nitroaniline (4-NA), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium chloride (CTAC), tween-20, PEG-4000 were acquired from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Tetrahydrofuran (THF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dichloromethane (DCM), methanol (MeOH) were obtained from Tianli Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). The ultrapure water (18 MΩ/cm) was acquired using a WaterPro water system (Axlwater Corporation, TY10AXLC1805-2, China) and used for all the experiments. All reagents belong to at least analytical grade and used as received.

UV–visible adsorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) and resonance light scattering (RLS) spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-2700 spectrophotometer using a quartz cuvette (light path 10 mm). 1H NMR spectra were obtained using a Bruker-AV400 spectrometer (400 MHz). Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) was performed on a Waters-2414 instrument using DMF as the eluent. Zeta potential and light scattering experiments were executed on a Malvern Nano ZS90 dynamic light scattering (DLS) spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100Plus microscope. Lifetime measurements were carried out on FLS980 Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). Photos of the cuvettes containing various solutions were taken by a Canon EOS R6 camera under a portable UV lamp (excited at 365 nm). The portable device for on-site testing was constructed by fitting a 365 nm UV Light connected to the USB port and cellphone holder into a black color small chamber employed as a sample holder. The RGB values of the captured images were analyzed using a color scanning application (Color Picker). The concentrations of PFOA and PFOS for the removal studies were determined using a liquid chromatography system (LCMS-8040, Shimadzu, Japan) with a quadrupole (QQQ) mass spectrometer equipped with an electrospray ionization source.

Firstly, monomers M1 and M2 were synthesized according to the procedures reported in literature (Hussain et al., 2020; Malik et al., 2016). Following this, the precursor brominated conjugated polymer PF-DBT-Br was prepared using Suzuki polymerization of monomers M1 and M2 and M3 with slight modifications in the earlier method (Cui et al., 2016). Briefly, after dissolving M1 (0.200 g, 0.308 mmol), M2 (0.254 g, 0.342 mmol) and M3 (0.016 g, 0.034 mmol) in 25 mL of tetrahydrofuran, the mixture was degassed by nitrogen bubbling followed by addition of 2 M K2CO3 (5 mL) under continuous stirring. After degassing for 30 min, Pd(0) catalyst (0.018 g, 0.016 mmol) was added and the mixture was stirred for 24 h at 70 °C under inert atmosphere. After removal of solvent under reduced pressure, the mixture was extracted three times with chloroform:brine (3:1), dried over MgSO4, and evaporated. The dried compound was dissolved in minimum chloroform, precipitated in excess methanol followed by washing with acetone thrice to obtain pure polymer PF-DBT-Br as pinkish-red-color product with a yield of 160 mg. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3): δ 8.21–7.39 (b, aromatic backbone), 3.36–3.15 (b, BrCH 2 (CH2)5), 2.22–0.70 (b, BrCH2 (CH 2)5). GPC in DMF: Mn = 4420 g mol−1, Mw = 7187 g mol−1, PDI-1.63.

PF-DBT-Br (50 mg) was then dissolved in 20 mL of CH2Cl2 and transferred to a reaction flask containing 6 mL of 1-methylimidazole. The mixture was subsequently stirred for two days at room temperature. After filtration, the crude product was repeatedly washed with CH2Cl2 to eliminate unreacted PF-DBT-Br followed by removal of solvent under reduced pressure. The dried product was dissolved in methanol, transferred to a dialysis bag with cut-off 3 kDa and subject to dialysis in methanol for 24 h to remove low-molecular-weight polymer. Finally, the product was dried in a vacuum oven to obtain the desired conjugated polymer PF-DBT-Im as purple-red-color compound with a yield of 35 mg. 1H NMR (400 MHz, d 6-DMSO): δ 9.30–9.01 (b, NCH CH N), δ 7.80–7.24 (b, aromatic H), δ 4.05–3.82 (b, NCH 2 (CH2)5), δ 3.76–3.58 (b, NCH 3), 2.25–0.40 (b, NCH2 (CH 2)5).

Magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4) were synthesized employing an improved hydrothermal method. Typically, FeCl3·6H2O (1.01 g), sodium acetate (2.70 g), and PEG-4000 (0.075 g) were dissolved in 15 mL of ethylene glycol. To acquire a homogenous solution, the mixture was first stirred at 200 °C for 5 h and then transferred to a 50 mL of teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for reaction at 200 °C for 10 h. Finally, the black product was separated magnetically, washed alternately with ultrapure water and ethanol, and then dried at 40 °C.

The magnetic adsorbent functionalized with amino groups and perfluorinated n-octyl chains (Fe3O4@NH2&F13) was prepared by a one-step sol-gel method. Briefly, 100 mg of Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed in 55 mL of ethanol-ultrapure water (2:3, v/v), followed by addition of 1.5 mL of 24 % ammonia under stirring. Then, 20 μL of TEOS, 30 μL of APTES, and 30 μL of PFOTES dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol were added dropwise and the reaction mixture was stirred continuously for 24 h. Finally, the obtained product Fe3O4@NH2&F13 was collected by an external magnet, washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol, and dried in vacuum at 50 °C.

The stock solution of PF-DBT-Im (1 mM) was prepared in DMSO and stored in the dark. All the studies were carried out after diluting PF-DBT-Im by ultrapure water from the stock solution according to the required concentration. The stock solutions of PFOA, PFOS, coexistences (E2, DOP, BPA, SM1, TCY, 4-NA), interfering surfactants (CTAB, CTAC, Tween-20, PEG-4000), PFASs analogues (SDS, SDBS, PFBA, PFBS, BA, BSA, OSA, OA), anions (Cl−, I−, AC−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, H2PO4 4−) and cations (Na+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ce2+) were prepared (1 mM) separately in ultrapure water and diluted as required.

1 mL of PF-DBT-Im solution (30 μM) in HEPES buffer (pH = 7.2) was placed in a 5 mL quartz cuvette (10 mm width). Then, the analytes (PFOA, PFOS) were introduced in portions and mixed properly keeping the total volume in the cuvette as about 1 mL. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature using an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, an emission range of 390–700 nm and a slit width of 5 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity ratio at 640 nm and 420 nm (I640/I420) were used to plot the calibration curves for PFOA and PFOS, respectively. A similar procedure was employed for monitoring the absorbance changes of PF-DBT-Im (30 μM) caused by the analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particles size and zeta potential measurements
ce:section.ce:para: For particle size measurement, PF-DBT-Im (30 μM) was dispersed in ultrapure water and DLS spectrum was recorded thrice (10 runs in each reading) to consider the average value. Similarly, the average of three readings was also considered for measurements of PF-DBT-Im/PFOA (30 μM/30 μM) and PF-DBT-Im/PFOS (30 μM/50 μM) samples. For determining the net charge, the zeta potential was recorded thrice for each sample and average was considered.

Lifetime experiments were performed to monitor the fluorescence lifetime decay of PF-DBT-Im in the absence and presence of PFOA/PFOS at an emission wavelength of donor (420 nm) and acceptor (640 nm) using a pulse laser excitation of 375 nm. The average value of lifetime (τavg) was determined using the below Eq. (1) after fitting the plots bi-exponentially-
(1)
τ avg

∑
i = 1

where ai and τi indicates pre-exponential factor and lifetime of ith species.
The average lifetime of PF-DBT-Im/PFOA and PF-DBT-Im/PFOS at emission wavelength of donor (420 nm) cannot be reported due to inaccurate fitting of the curve.

Tap water, pool water, and river water were selected as actual samples to verify the applicability of this method. Tap water and pool water were taken from the innovation harbor campus of Xi'an Jiaotong University, and the river water was taken from the Weihe River (Xi'an, Shaanxi, China). The water samples were pretreated by heating and adding an excess of Ba2+(Cl−) to eliminate the interference of organic solvents and surfactants SDS/SDBS. Following this, the water samples were spiked with known concentrations of PFOA and PFOS, separately to prepare the stock solutions. Different volumes of PFOA and PFOS-spiked water samples from the stock solution were added to the solution of PF-DBT-Im (30 μM) in HEPES buffer (pH = 7.2) and fluorescence spectra were monitored. The results were compared with the standard calibration curves for estimation.

After completing the fluorescence detection based on the interactions between PF-DBT-Im (30 μM, 1 mL) and PFOA (30 μM) or PFOS (50 μM), 2 mg of Fe3O4@NH2&F13 was added to the above mixed solution followed by ultrasonic dispersion and incubation for 30 min. Then, the adsorbents were separated magnetically and the supernatant was filtered with a disposable nylon membrane (0.22 μm) followed by direct injection into LC-MS instrument for analysis without any preconcentration step. The chromatographic separation of the compounds was achieved on a YMC-Triart C18 column (5 μm, 150 mm × 4.6 mm). The column temperature was 30 °C and the optimized mobile phase was methanol-5 mM aqueous ammonium acetate (90/10, v/v) delivered at a flow rate of 0.300 mL min−1, and the injection volume was 1 μL. MS detection was performed in a negative ion electrospray mode, while, single ion monitoring (SIM) mode was employed to quantify PFASs with the detection ion of m/z = 413 (PFOA) and 499 (PFOS), respectively. The ESI source values were as follows: capillary voltage-3500 V and nebulizer pressure-35 psi. Dry gas flow rate and temperature were 15 L min−1 and 300 °C, respectively.

In order to avoid contamination, all the appliances used in the process of sample collection, transportation and treatment do not employ polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) or other fluoropolymer materials. Procedure blanks, and solvent blanks were collected and analyzed in every sample batch. The blank sample of pure solvent was analyzed before the determination of each group and no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of PF-DBT-Im was detected. Procedural blank samples also showed no change in the fluorescence intensity of PF-DBT-Im. Each sample was subjected to three replicate measurements and the standard deviation was <5 %. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQs) were defined as 3σ/slope and 10σ/slope, respectively, and the results are listed in Table S1.

During LC-MS analysis, all glass vials were soaked overnight in the alkali and baked at 500 °C for 4 h to remove possible residual compounds. The LC system was cleaned thoroughly with mobile phase of 90 % methanol. The blank samples of pure solvents were analyzed before the determination of each group, and no target chemicals were detected. The procedure blank sample also shows that no target chemical was detected. A 7-point standard calibration curve was established for the quantification of PFOA/PFOS with correlation coefficients (R 2) exceeding 0.99. LODs and LOQs were defined as an instrumental signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1 and 10:1, respectively. Detailed values are listed in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and optical properties of PF-DBT-Im
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic method for cationic conjugated polymer PF-DBT-Im is shown in Fig. 1a. Polyfluorene (PF) derivative PF-DBT-Im with co-doping (5 %) of 1,4-dithienylbenzothiadiazole (DBT) segments was prepared by Suzuki polymerization. Firstly, the monomers M1, M2, and M3 were taken in the ratio of 0.45:0.50:0.05 and copolymerized by Suzuki polymerization to yield PF-DBT-Br. The molecular weight (Mw) of precursor polymer PF-DBT-Br was found as 7187 g mol−1 with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 1.63. 1-methylimidazole was then attached onto the side chains of PF-DBT-Br using post-functionalization strategy to acquire desired polymer PF-DBT-Im. All the corresponding characterization spectra of products including NMR and GPC are shown in the supporting information (Figs. S1–S3). PF-DBT-Im exhibits a relative photoluminescence quantum yield (Φ) of 10 % in water using quinine sulphate (Φr = 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4) as reference. The absorption spectrum of PF-DBT-Im in water showed two absorption peaks at 380 and 540 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b), which are characteristic of π-π* transition (Cui et al., 2016) of fluorene and DBT segments along the backbone. Meanwhile, upon excitation at 370 nm, the PL spectra of PF-DBT-Im in water displays (Fig. 1b) a dominant band centered at ~420 nm (fluorene segments) and a less pronounced band at ~640 nm (DBT segments). Efficient spectral overlap between the fluorescence of fluorene and absorption of DBT infers the chance of FRET from fluorene to DBT. To affirm this, the excitation spectra of PF-DBT-Im were measured at an emission wavelength of 420 and 640 nm, respectively (Fig. 1c). Two excitation peaks were observed at 380 and 530 nm for DBT emission (640 nm). The excitation peak of 380 nm (640 nm) is almost similar to absorption peak of fluorene (Fig. 1b) and excitation peak of fluorene (420 nm) (Fig. 1c), indicating the existence of intramolecular FRET from fluorene to DBT. Nevertheless, the efficiency of intramolecular FRET remained very low and the emission peak of 420 is dominant due to low fraction of DBT units along the polymer backbone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical detection of PFOA and PFOS
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the emission of PF-DBT-Im was monitored prior to sensing experiments by varying the pH of the solution from 2 to 12. The results demonstrated that PF-DBT-Im exhibits reasonable emission at pH <7.5 (Fig. S4), therefore, buffer solution with pH 7.2 was selected as the optimum measurement condition. For realizing optical sensing, the change in fluorescence intensity of PF-DBT-Im was then monitored in the presence of PFOA and PFOS as shown in Fig. 2a, c. As the concentration of analytes increases, the fluorene emission band centered at 420 nm was gradually decreased, while the band centered at 640 nm increased slightly at the same time. Notably, >90 % reduction in the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm was observed after adding total 30 μM of PFOA and 50 μM of PFOS, respectively. As a result, the color of PF-DBT-Im solution changed from blue to magenta after the addition of PFOA/PFOS, which was clearly visible under irradiation by a 365 nm UV lamp (insets of Fig. 2a, c). In addition, the ratio of emission intensities I640/I420 displayed a good linear relationship with increasing concentrations of PFOA and PFOS (Fig. S5) which manifests the fluorescence based ratiometric estimation of PFOA/PFOS. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated using 3σ/slope method (Hussain et al., 2016) (Fig. S5) was found to be 6.12 nM (PFOA) and 14.3 nM (PFOS), confirming reasonable sensitivity of PF-DBT-Im for PFOA and PFOS. To study the efficiency of quenching, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (Ksv) values were also calculated through linear fitting of the curve between change in fluorescence intensity against quencher concentration (Hussain et al., 2016) (Fig. 2b, d). The Ksv values were found to be significantly high as 6.26 × 104 M−1 (PFOA) and 4.84 × 104 M−1 (PFOS), suggesting remarkable response of PF-DBT-Im towards PFOA and PFOS.

To elucidate the underlying sensing mechanism, the complexation process between PF-DBT-Im and PFOA/PFOS was investigated by UV–vis spectroscopy, zeta potential measurements, particle size analysis, morphological studies, and lifetime studies. PF-DBT-Im bearing positive charge and hydrophobic backbone exhibit a great tendency to bind with amphiphilic molecules PFOA/PFOS via electrostatic and hydrophobic intercations. As shown in Fig. 3a, b, the absorption peaks of PF-DBT-Im at 380 and 530 nm displayed bathochromic shifts of 9 and 14 nm, respectively after addition of PFOA. On other hand, comparatively less bathochromic shifts of 2 and 4 nm were observed for PFOS. These bathochromic shifts in the absorption spectra can be ascribed to the aggregation of polymer chains caused by complexation between PF-DBT-Im and PFOA/PFOS possibly via electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions (Hussain et al., 2015). To confirm this, change in zeta potential and particle size was also observed. Upon addition of PFOA and PFOS, PF-DBT-Im demonstrates reduction in its net charge from +27.57 mV to +2.18 mV (PFOA) and −8.81 mV (PFOS) (Fig. 3c), signifying that the cationic polymer PF-DBT-Im exhibited strong binding affinity towards negatively charged PFOA/PFOS. The lower zeta potential value of PF-DBT-Im/PFOS than PF-DBT-Im/PFOA can be ascribed to the lower pKa of PFOS (pKa = −3.27) in contrast to PFOA (pKa = 2.8) (Yang et al., 2021). DLS spectra further support the formation of PF-DBT-Im/PFOA and PF-DBT-Im/PFOS aggregates, since, the average particle size of PF-DBT-Im (151 nm) increased after the addition of PFOA (417 nm) and PFOS (314 nm), respectively (Fig. 3d).

To shed more light on complexation process, TEM images were recorded. It is revealed that PF-DBT-Im exists as quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a uniform size around 80 nm (Fig. S6a). After binding with PFOA and PFOS, the irregular aggregates were observed for PF-DBT-Im/PFOA complex (Fig. S6b), while staggered networks were obtained for PF-DBT-Im/PFOS complex (Fig. S6c). Thus, it is clear from the aforementioned results, that PF-DBT-Im undergoes strong aggregation after binding with PFOA/PFOS, enabling fluorescence ratiometric monitoring of PFOA and PFOS.

Finally, to confirm the existence of interchain FRET induced by binding of PFOA and PFOS with PF-DBT-Im, lifetime studies were performed. It is well-known that fluorescence lifetime of the donor molecule is usually decreased while acceptor molecule is increased in case of FRET (Hussain et al., 2016). Thus, the lifetime decay of PF-DBT-Im was monitored at emission of the donor (fluorene-420 nm) as well as acceptor (DBT-640 nm) in the absence and presence of PFOA and PFOS, respectively. Interestingly, PF-DBT-Im demonstrates significant reduction in the lifetime of fluorene and simultaneous increment in the lifetime of DBT in the presence of PFOA and PFOS, respectively (Fig. S6d, e, Table S3). Thus, it can be concluded that binding of PFOA/PFOS induced aggregation of PF-DBT-Im which ultimately facilitate interchain FRET enabling drastic color change of the solution and promote ratiometric detection.

For practical application, the selectivity of the probe towards particular analyte is crucial and necessary. Thus, the selectivity of PF-DBT-Im was investigated using various common analytes (surfactants, anions, cations, analogues, etc.) typically exist in natural water systems as shown in Fig. 4 . Most common cations, anions, surfactants and coexistences usually found in environmental water did not affect the fluorescence of PF-DBT-Im (Fig. 4a, b). Interestingly, BA and BSA, the two alkyl acids with shorter carbon chains and OA and OSA which have high structural similarity (except the absence of F atoms) with PFOA and PFOS also showed insignificant responses towards PF-DBT-Im (Fig. 4c). This indicates that PFOA/PFOS being more hydrophobic (Chen et al., 2022) than BA/BSA and OA/OSA due to the presence of longer alkyl chain and/or F atoms endorse more favorable hydrophobic interactions with PF-DBT-Im driven by electrostatic force and ultimately deliver fluorescence ratiometric response. Likewise, other perfluoroalkyl substances such as PFBA and PFBS which carry relatively shorter chains than PFOA/PFOS were also ineffectual towards PF-DBT-Im (Fig. 4c). However, SDS and SDBS, the two most common anionic surfactants found in environmental water significantly affect the emission of PF-DBT-Im as shown in Fig. 4c. Note that the aggregation of cationic conjugated polymers by these two anionic surfactants bearing very long alkyl chains is a common phenomenon and exhaustively reported in literature (Hussain et al., 2015). Thus, we also put efforts to avoid the interference of SDS and SDBS by using barium salt (BaCl2) which possess great tendency (He et al., 2020) to bind and eliminate SDS and SDBS from the water. As shown in Fig. S7f, SDS and Ba2+ could form stable complex and turn the solution turbid, resulting in a significant enhancement of RLS signal. Intriguingly, these particles can be easily removed by filtration using 0.22 μm membrane prior to detection. On contrary, no change in RLS signal was observed (Fig. S7f) after treatment of Ba2+ with PFOA or PFOS and the solutions remained transparent. Encouraging from this observation, we turn to fluorescence spectroscopy to monitor the exclusion of SDS interference by Ba2+. As depicted in the Fig. S7a–c, all PFOA, PFOS and SDS affected the fluorescence intensity of PF-DBT-Im in the similar manner. After addition of Ba2+, the intensity of PF-DBT-Im/SDS was recovered by ~90 %, while, the signals of PF-DBT-Im/PFOA and PF-DBT-Im/PFOS remained almost same indicating exclusive property of Ba2+ to bind with SDS. Note that the interference of SDBS during detection can also be removed in similar fashion (Fig. S8) by using Ba2+. Finally, to comprehend whether the current detection system can also discriminate PFOA/PFOS from common surfactants like SDS, we performed the detection under mixed environment by adding SDS + PFOA and SDS + PFOS to the solution of PF-DBT-Im followed by addition of Ba2+. Fascinatingly, the detection of PFOA/PFOS was also achieved successfully even in mixed conditions (Fig. S7d, e) facilitating discriminative sensing. Thus, it is concluded that PF-DBT-Im can be utilized as a selective fluorescence ratiometric probe for discriminative detection of PFOA/PFOS even in complicated environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PFOA and PFOS in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Trace monitoring of persistent organic pollutants PFOA and PFOS in natural water systems is indispensable for protecting environmental and public health. The detection studies were also carried out in real water samples using tap water, lake water and river water. The quantitative analysis of PFOA/PFOS in pretreated water samples was performed using a standard recovery method as shown in Table S4. The recoveries for PFOA and PFOS were found in the range of 90.6–106.0 % and 84.4–111.6 % with relative standard deviation (RSD) <5 % and <7 %, respectively. These results confirm that the proposed method is sensibly accurate, precise and exhibit promising potential for the detection of PFOA and PFOS in complex water environment.

To transform the current detection system into a convenient platform for realizing on-site and real-time detection of PFOA/PFOS, a simple and cheap portable device consisting of a sample chamber, mobile phone holder and a portable UV light is constructed as shown in Fig. 5a. Different samples of PF-DBT-Im containing variable concentrations of PFOA and PFOS were inserted into the chamber and irradiated by UV light. A smartphone was used to capture the image of each sample followed by scanning of color by an easily accessible application (APP) “Color Picker”. Finally, the values of the red (R) and blue (B) color were acquired for each measurement and repeated three times. According to the ratio of R/B, the linear calibration curves were obtained for the quantitative estimation of PFOA and PFOS (Fig. 5b and c) with a correlation coefficient (R2) value of 0.9826 (PFOA) and 0.9887 (PFOS), respectively. Thus, the emerging pollutants PFOA/PFOS can be monitored at desirable locations instantly utilizing an easily fabricated, simple and convenient portable device equipped with a smartphone.

In order to remove hazardous substances PFOA and PFOS from water bodies and avoid secondary pollution caused by detection, a new type of magnetic adsorbent (Fe3O4@NH2&F13) functionalized with amino groups and perfluorinated n-octyl chains was fabricated and used (Figs. 6 and S9). Fe3O4@NH2&F13 exhibits the following two exciting features. (1) It has a strong tendency to adsorb PFOA/PFOS through electrostatic and fluorine-fluorine hydrophobic interactions. (2) It can be easily separated after adsorption of PFOA/PFOS by an external magnetic field. Since Fe3O4@NH2&F13 do not show a characteristic absorption peak, the absorption band of PF-DBT-Im was employed to study the magnetic removal of PFOA and PFOS by Fe3O4@NH2&F13.

As shown in Fig. 7a, the typical absorption peaks of PF-DBT-Im were reduced after incubation of PF-DBT-Im/PFOA and PF-DBT-Im/PFOS with Fe3O4@NH2&F13 followed by magnetic removal. In contrast, almost no change was observed when Fe3O4@NH2&F13 was incubated with only PF-DBT-Im and subjected to magnetic separation. Hence, conjugated polymer PF-DBT-Im not only assists in the ratiometric fluorescence detection of PFOA/PFOS but also helps in the examination of separation process by simply monitoring absorption peak and color change of solution from light pink to colorless under day light and magenta to lavender under UV light as shown in Fig. 7b. These results demonstrated that PF-DBT-Im/PFOA and PF-DBT-Im/PFOS complexes could be efficiently removed by Fe3O4@NH2&F13 through favorable interactions between them, and the absorption peak and color of PF-DBT-Im can be utilized as a tool for monitoring separation of PFOA/PFOS. It is worthy to mention that the development of such multifunctional systems which can visually detect as well as remove PFOA/PFOS from environmental water is highly challenging and rarely exist. To reconfirm the removal of PFOA and PFOS along with PF-DBT-Im, the supernatant was collected after magnetic separation and analyzed by LC-MS. As shown in Fig. 7c, d, the LC-MS peaks of PFOA and PFOS showed drastic reduction after the incubation of Fe3O4@NH2&F13 with PF-DBT-Im/PFOA or PF-DBT-Im/PFOS, further confirming that PFOA/PFOS may act as a bridge between PF-DBT-Im and Fe3O4@NH2&F13 and separated successfully with high removal efficiency of 95.68 % (PFOA) and 96.99 % (PFOS), respectively. This affirms that magnetic adsorbent Fe3O4@NH2&F13 is capable of removing the emerging pollutants PFOA and PFOS from the environmental water efficiently and rapidly and also superior to some previously reported adsorbents (Table S5). Thus, it is believed that magnetic adsorbent Fe3O4@NH2&F13 together with conjugated polymer PF-DBT-Im is capable of removing the pollutants PFOA and PFOS from the environmental water efficiently, thereby providing remediation of water pollution.

To highlight the supremacy of the proposed method, we compared our work with some previously reported fluorometric sensors for the detection of PFOA and PFOS, as shown in Table 1 . It is apparent that the current ratiometric detection method based on signal amplified conjugated polymer PF-DBT-Im is better in terms of sensitivity, resulting in a lower detection limit. In addition, compared with most other fluorometric approaches, our method can achieve on-site and real-time detection using smartphone integrated portable device and remove PF-DBT-Im/PFOA and PF-DBT-Im/PFOS simply through magnetic separation without causing secondary pollution to the environment.

In conclusion, an efficient strategy for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of persistent organic pollutants PFOA and PFOS is developed on the basis of aggregation induced interchain FRET in conjugated polymer PF-DBT-Im. Taking advantage of this strategy, we not only realize the quantitative and hypersensitive detection of PFOA/PFOS in complex water environment, but also achieved their efficient removal via simple magnetic adsorption and separation procedure employing specially designed Fe3O4@NH2&F13 nanoparticles as smart adsorbent. In addition, we have also developed a smartphone-assisted portable detection device that is capable of performing real-time and on-site estimation of PFOA/PFOS. Thus, this work offers a new promising strategy for the detection as well as remediation of water pollution.
